coredata.dc:title: Peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activated by magnetic Fe3O4 doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light: DFT calculations and mechanism analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Peng
Zhang, Xingzong
Cheng, Zhiliang
Xu, Qian
Zhang, Xuan
Liu, Yuan
Qiu, Facheng
coredata.dc:description: Currently, photo-assisted peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation has received more and more attention in the degradation of wastewater pollutants. In this paper, the Fe3O4@CQDs magnetic nanocatalyst was successfully prepared by modifying α-Fe2O3, and Rhodamine B (RhB) was degraded by activating PMS under visible light. Moreover, in order to explore the reason why Fe3O4@CQDs was more beneficial to the activation of PMS than α-Fe2O3 at the microscopic level and reaction mechanism, the corresponding DFT calculations were performed. The results revealed that the degradation efficiency of RhB reached 98% in 35 min. Subsequently, the quenching experiments and EPR tests jointly verified that SO4•-, •OH, O2•- free radical pathway and 1O2, h + non-free radical pathway participated in the degradation of RhB in the Fe3O4@CQDs + PMS + light system. The adsorption energy reached −5.247 eV with Fe3O4@CQDs adsorbed PMS by DFT calculation. The electron enrichment and charge redistribution can be apparently observed during the adsorption of PMS at Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalyst. It was confirmed that the embedding of CQDs effectively regulated the electron transfer efficiency at the Fe3O4@CQDs interface to promote the improvement of electrical conductivity. It was more conducive to forming Fe(Ⅲ) to Fe(Ⅱ) cycles, and then the ability of the catalyst to activate PMS was improved.

head.ce:title: Peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activated by magnetic Fe3O4 doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light: DFT calculations and mechanism analysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Currently, photo-assisted peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation has received more and more attention in the degradation of wastewater pollutants. In this paper, the Fe3O4@CQDs magnetic nanocatalyst was successfully prepared by modifying α-Fe2O3, and Rhodamine B (RhB) was degraded by activating PMS under visible light. Moreover, in order to explore the reason why Fe3O4@CQDs was more beneficial to the activation of PMS than α-Fe2O3 at the microscopic level and reaction mechanism, the corresponding DFT calculations were performed. The results revealed that the degradation efficiency of RhB reached 98% in 35 min. Subsequently, the quenching experiments and EPR tests jointly verified that SO4•-, •OH, O2•- free radical pathway and 1O2, h + non-free radical pathway participated in the degradation of RhB in the Fe3O4@CQDs + PMS + light system. The adsorption energy reached −5.247 eV with Fe3O4@CQDs adsorbed PMS by DFT calculation. The electron enrichment and charge redistribution can be apparently observed during the adsorption of PMS at Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalyst. It was confirmed that the embedding of CQDs effectively regulated the electron transfer efficiency at the Fe3O4@CQDs interface to promote the improvement of electrical conductivity. It was more conducive to forming Fe(Ⅲ) to Fe(Ⅱ) cycles, and then the ability of the catalyst to activate PMS was improved.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the development of the textile industry provides us with plenty of conveniences in our daily lives. Nevertheless, the inability of textile manufacturing to properly deal with water pollution, which is caused by wastewater pollutants, is one of the major challenges facing the world. Dye wastewater brings a lot of unfavorable drawbacks including high COD, complex composition, and difficult decolorization. (Al-Tohamy et al., 2022). Furthermore, the pollutants of dye wastewater have been found to contain a wide range of carcinogenic with mercury, chromium, cadmium, lead, and heavy metals (Chen et al., 2023a; Kishor et al., 2021; Rai et al., 2022). Once the pollutants are enriched through the food chain, enter the human body and eventually endanger health. Thus, the effective treatment of refractory pollutants in dye wastewater has become one of the major and difficult projects in the construction of ecological civilization.

When it comes to the several available advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) applications in wastewater treatment, peroxymonosulfate (PMS) as one of the main sources of sulfate radical (SO4 −•) method has attracted more and more attention from researchers because of its unique advantages (Fan et al., 2022; Tan et al., 2017; Tian et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2022a). That is to say, SO4 −• has been regarded as a powerful oxidant with an oxidation potential of 2.5–3.1 V, which is even higher than the 1.9–2.7 V oxidation potential of •OH (Chen, F. et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022). Besides, the half-life of SO4 −• (30–40 μs) is much higher than that of •OH (<1 μs), which means the SO4 −• reacts better with organic pollutants (Hu and Long, 2016; Ji et al., 2019). However, since only PMS produce SO4 −• inefficiently, it needs to be activated. The traditional activation methods generally have some problems, including heat activation with high pH dependence, UV with high energy consumption and expensive cost (Chen et al., 2023b). Nowadays, transition metal ions have been proven effective methods that can activate PMS to generate SO4 −•. In particular, the activation of PMS by iron ions has been widely investigated due to its availability, non-toxicity, low cost and environmental friendliness (Karim et al., 2020). The traditional iron-based catalysts also have many shortcomings, such as weak catalytic activity, low atomic utilization efficiency, and slow conversion of Fe(Ⅲ) to Fe(Ⅱ). The above situation results in poor reusability.

It is a feasible strategy for supporting carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on Fe-based catalysts to activate PMS. CQDs is a new-fangled type of carbon-based nanostructured material that fluoresces brightly (Khan et al., 2022). It is made up of unique, quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with ultrafine particles that are less than 10 nm in size. Besides, it is composed of carbon atoms in sp2 and sp3 hybrid and connected with oxygen-containing functional groups (Huang et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2021). Supplementarily, the π conjugated structure of CQDs acts as an electron acceptor and contributes to the transfer of photogenerated electrons, it also can capture and utilize light in a wider wavelength range (Nie et al., 2018). It has been applied with simple preparation, non-toxicity and excellent electron transmission performance in various fields (Huang et al., 2020). So far, many studies have reported that CQDs-supported metal oxides respond to photocatalysis, which can enhance photocatalytic efficiency. For instance, TiO2 (Gong et al., 2022; Yu et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2019), ZnO (Ja'Farawy et al., 2022), g-C3N4 (Huang et al., 2022; Preeyanghaa et al., 2022), CuO (Muthulingam et al., 2021), etc. However, a single CQDs-supported metal oxide photocatalysis has the drawbacks of a high electron-hole recombination rate with a low degradation efficiency. Thus, coupling visible light photocatalysis with oxidants has been reported as a promising degradation method with other significant advantages such as high efficiency, high reusability, and low secondary pollution. CQDs can effectively capture photogenerated electrons, and this situation results in promoting charge separation and transferring to enhance the Fe(Ⅲ)/Fe(Ⅱ) cycling rate. Secondly, the introduction of CQDs greatly inhibits the photogenerated electron-hole recombination and promotes the carrier separation of PMS decomposition and photodegradation.

In this study, the Fe3O4@CQDs magnetic nanocatalyst was successfully prepared by the hydrothermal method. Then, the properties of morphology, structure and chemical composition were systematically investigated by XRD, FT-IR, BET, UV–Vis, PL, SEM/EDS, TEM/HRTEM, VSM and XPS. Meanwhile, the catalytic performance of Fe3O4@CQDs magnetic nanocatalyst was evaluated by using Rhodamine B (RhB) to simulate the degradation efficiency of dye wastewater. The effect of single process parameters on the degradation efficiency of RhB was evaluated. Furthermore, the reusable performance of the Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalyst was researched. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) were explored to better reveal the degradation processes within the Fe3O4@CQDs + PMS + light system. Ultimately, the reason why Fe3O4@CQDs is beneficial to the activation of PMS is analyzed by corresponding DFT calculations.
The chemicals and material information are presented in the supplementary material S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis of α-Fe2O3 and Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The α-Fe2O3 nanocatalyst was synthesized by hydrothermal method, and the detailed process is expressed in the supplementary material S2.
The CQDs preparation is presented in the supplementary material S3.

The typical preparation process of Fe3O4@CQDs magnetic nanocatalyst is as follows. 1.3650 g ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) was slowly added into 35 mL ethanol (C2H5OH) and 2.5 mL deionized water. Subsequently, 2.789 g anhydrous sodium acetate (CH3COONa) and 0.6065 g glucose (C6H12O6) were added. The resulting solution was reacted in a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene high-pressure reactor at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the samples were centrifuged and washed three times with ethanol and deionized water, respectively. Finally, the sample was dried under vacuum at 50–60 °C and the nanocatalysts we needed were obtained. The catalyst preparation synthesis is presented in Fig. 1 .
The detailed characterizations of catalysts are presented in the supplementary material S4.

The experiment was conducted in 500 mL glass beakers containing a 300 mL solution of RhB, which was continuously stirred by a magnetic stirrer at approximately 200 rpm and irradiated with a xenon lamp (λ > 420 nm, 300W). The diagram of the experimental device is presented in the Supplementary Material Fig. S1.

Specifically, a different single process parameters influence was explored through catalyst dosages, PMS concentrations, RhB initial concentrations, reaction temperatures, initial pHs, coexisting anions, HA and different pollutants. Subsequently, the reusability performance of Fe3O4@CQDs was investigated. Ultimately, ROS was verified by the addition of quenching reagents.

5 mL aliquots were taken out from the glass beaker every 5 min during the experimental treatment and passed through a filter membrane with a pore size of 0.22 μm to get a clear and transparent solution of RhB degradation. Then the absorbance was determined by the spectrophotometer at 554 nm.

The degradation rate of RhB can be calculated by the equation below (Yan et al., 2022).
(1) Degradation rate = (C 0-C t)/C 0 × 100%
(2) ln(C 0/C t) = kt
Where C 0 and C t are the concentration of RhB at time 0 and t, respectively, k is the kinetic constant of the first-order reaction.

To further investigate the catalytic performance of the catalyst, the DFT calculation method was performed in this study. The DFT calculation method is in the supplementary material S5. The first step in the DFT calculation was to simplify the calculation model, which involved constructing a model of the catalytic system that contained a cluster of iron atoms, oxygen atoms, and CQDs. The optimized model was then subjected to DFT calculations, which were used to extract a variety of key data points, including the adsorption energy and reaction energy. The adsorption energy of reactant molecules on the catalyst surface was calculated to determine the strength of the interaction between them and the surface, while the reaction energy provided insight into the thermodynamics of the catalytic reaction. The results of the DFT calculations presented the relationships between various catalytic properties and the structure of the catalyst. Overall, the DFT calculations provided valuable insights into the catalytic performance of the Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalyst and helped to guide further experimental work aimed at improving its efficiency and selectivity.

The crystal structure and phase composition of the catalyst were analyzed by XRD. The XRD patterns of the unmodified catalyst correspond to characteristic peaks of Fe2O3 (JCPDS, NO. 330664). Two broad peaks visible in Fig. 2 (a) match the (100) and (110) crystal planes. The distinct diffraction peaks at 2θ values corresponding to 33.15°, 35.61°, 49.48°, 54.09°, 62.45° and 63.99°, which are assigned to the typical planes of (104), (110), (024), (116), (214) and (300) of the newly synthesized material and are consistent with the XRD patterns of magnetic Fe3O4 (JCPDS, NO. 750033).

FT-IR spectra analysis was for further investigation to determine the difference between CQDs, Fe3O4, α-Fe2O3 and Fe3O4@CQDs (Fig. 2(b)). For CQDs, 3411 cm−1,2933 cm−1,1732 cm−1,1638 cm−1,1174 cm−1 is consistent with O–H, C–H, C=O, C=C, and C–O bonds. α-Fe2O3 and Fe3O4 are only detected at 500-800 cm−1 vibrations, which are considered to be Fe–O bonds (Chen et al., 2023b). The tensile vibration at 3661 cm−1 is associated to be the O–H bond of water produced on the Fe3O4@CQDs. The tensile vibrations of about 1732 cm−1, 1491 cm−1 and 1174 cm−1 are related to C=O bonds, C–C bonds and C–O bonds respectively (Han et al., 2020a; Zhou et al., 2022). Besides, the vibrations between 800 cm−1 and 500 cm−1 are confirmed to be Fe–O bonds (Wu et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2022).

Optical properties of Fe3O4, α-Fe2O3 and Fe3O4@CQDs were discussed via UV–vis absorption spectra with the results depicted in Fig. 2(c). Fe3O4 shows a poor photoadsorption range, while α-Fe2O3 emerges with intense absorption for visible light, revealing its wide light adsorption range. After a modification operation, Fe3O4@CQDs retains the characteristics of α-Fe2O3 with a wide photoadsorption range under visible light. Furthermore, the Eg value of Fe3O4@CQDs declines to 1.71 eV, and the narrow band gap can greatly strengthen the utilization efficiency of visible light (Fig. 2(d)). Thus, the above situation can promote the generation of photoinduced carriers (Chen et al., 2021). It suggests that Fe3O4@CQDs might have higher photocatalytic activity for the target reaction.

The photogenic carrier transfer behavior was analyzed by photoluminescence. The PL spectra of α-Fe2O3 and Fe3O4@CQDs are demonstrated in Fig. 2(e). The α-Fe2O3 exhibits a weak photoluminescence signal due to its low response to light. Whereas CQDs promotes carrier separation and hinder recombination problems thanks to excellent properties such as electron storage. It can not only inhibit electron-hole but also accelerate electron transfer and improve the Fe(Ⅲ)/Fe(Ⅱ) cycling rate (Huang et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2019). And then, the catalytic efficiency of Fe3O4@CQDs activates PMS system can be enhanced.

In addition, the specific surface area is a key factor affecting catalytic performance (Huang et al., 2018), the α-Fe2O3 and Fe3O4@CQDs were characterized by BET analysis, the SBET, pore volume, and average pore presented in the Supplementary Material Table S1. The SBET of Fe3O4@CQDs (52.0603 m2/g) is higher than that of α-Fe2O3 (26.0364 m2/g), and the larger surface area can provide more active sites for the reaction, evidencing that the composite has excellent properties. Furthermore, the adsorption and desorption curves of catalysts were determined by the N2 adsorption method (Fig. 2(f) and (g)). The adsorption-desorption curve of α-Fe2O3 belongs to type III isotherm and has an H4 type hysteresis loop, which means the irregular mesoporous structure formed by aggregated particles (Liu et al., 2017). The adsorption-desorption curve of Fe3O4@CQDs belongs to type IV isotherm and also has an H4 hysteresis loop, which indicates that a large number of irregular fracture voids are formed in the Fe3O4@CQDs. The morphology and structure of these pores are consistent with the results of the SEM analysis. Fig. 2(h) and (i) show that with the increase in pore size, the void volume overall decreases. That means better surface properties of Fe3O4@CQDs have been obtained.
These results both manifest that the performance of the Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalyst is better after modifications.

Fig. 3 (a) and (b) present the SEM images of α-Fe2O3 at different magnifications, the particle is well dispersed and the shape is regular cylindrical with a surface relatively smooth, which has a microcrystalline structure with a size of 1 μm. The SEM images of the Fe3O4@CQDs are displayed in Fig. 3(c) and (d), which are evenly dispersed and irregular spherical particles with the surface relatively rough. Fig. 3(e) shows the mapping images energy spectrum of the Fe3O4@CQDs. Fe, C and O elements are uniformly distributed.

As presented in Fig. 3(h), the lattice fringe spacing of 0.25 nm corresponds to the (110) crystal plane of α-Fe2O3, indicating that this plane is the dominantly exposed crystal plane (Yan et al., 2022). The lattice spacing in Fe3O4 is 0.25 nm corresponding to the (311) plane, as displayed in Fig. 3(k). In addition, the fringe spacing of CQDs is 0.21 nm, which is related to the CQDs (100) plane (Fig. 3(n)). The particles with lattice fringe spacing of 0.22 nm in the Fe3O4@CQDs correspond to the (100) planes of the carbon quantum dots (Zhang et al., 2020). Meanwhile, the lattice spacing of 0.25 nm and 0.15 nm correspond to the (311) and (440) planes of Fe3O4 (Fig. 3(q)), respectively (JCPDF: 75-0033). The above results fully demonstrated the successful preparation of nanocomposite catalysts.

The corresponding control experiments investigated the degradation efficiency of RhB in different systems, Fig. 4 (a) is the degradation rate, and Fig. 4(b) is the reaction rate constant k. The degradation rate of RhB was less than 3.7% in the Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalyst alone. It indicated that the catalyst had limited adsorption capacity. The degradation rate increased slightly to only 8.2% in the Fe3O4@CQDs + light system, which implied the system could not be photodegradated and needed to be activated by the oxidant. About 19.5% of RhB was degraded with only PMS, this phenomenon can be explained that the oxidation capacity of PMS was weak with no other catalysts activated. Besides, approximately 23.5% and 45.7% of RhB was degraded in the α-Fe2O3 + PMS + light system/Fe3O4 + PMS + light system, it attributed to the catalyst performance being weak without modification. Moreover, the degradation rate of Fe3O4@CQDs + PMS in the absence of visible light was far lower than in the presence of visible light. The situation is because the CQDs-modified catalyst has plenty of merits, such as effectively capturing photogenerated electrons, improving charge separation, enhancing photocatalytic performance, and strengthening the Fe(Ⅲ)/Fe(Ⅱ) cycling rate. Thereby it improved the degradation efficiency in the Fe3O4@CQDs + PMS + light system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of process parameters on RhB degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The degradation efficiency of RhB was investigated by different single process parameters experiments in the Fe3O4@CQDs + PMS + light system, including catalyst dosages, PMS concentrations, RhB initial concentrations, reaction temperatures and initial pHs, respectively. The change in degradation efficiency over time is shown in Fig. 5 , and the reaction rate constants k is presented in the Supplementary Material Fig. S2.

The catalyst plays a crucial role in providing active sites, the dosage of which directly affects the degradation efficiency (Chen et al., 2019). As illustrated in Fig. 5(a), with the increase of Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalyst dosage from 0 to 110 mg, the degradation efficiency of RhB first gradually increased and then tended to be stable. Simultaneously, the reaction rate constant k increased from 0.0074 min−1 to 0.1253 min−1 with the same conditions for 35 min, this phenomenon was because Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalyst exposed more active sites to produce ROS with PMS (Ma et al., 2019). Besides, the reason for the constant degradation efficiency since excessive catalysts cannot significantly improve the generation efficiency of ROS with no additional PMS activation. On the other hand, excessive catalysts shield light absorption and reduce light utilization.

The concentration of PMS is a significant factor affecting organic pollutant degradation. Thus, the concentration of PMS was researched as demonstrated in Fig. 5(b). The catalytic efficiency and degradation rate were not satisfied with no PMS in the system, indicating that ROS by PMS plays an essential role in the degradation of pollutants. The degradation efficiency increased significantly with the concentration of PMS increasing from 1 mM to 5 mM. It is that an increase in the concentration of PMS prominently reinforced the contact of more PMS with the catalyst which promoted more SO4 −•. However, it showed a downward trend with the concentration of PMS further increased. The reason for this result is that excessive PMS would quench SO4 −• and •OH, leading to a slowdown of the RhB degradation efficiency, as shown in Eqs. (3) and (4) (Hussain et al., 2017). Therefore, 5 mM was determined as a reasonable PMS concentration.
(3) SO4 −• + HSO5 − → SO4 − + HSO5 −•
(4) •OH + HSO5 − → H2O + HSO5 −•

Furthermore, the effect of RhB initial concentrations was studied in the Fe3O4@CQDs + PMS + light system (Fig. 5(c)). The degradation efficiency decreased gradually with the RhB initial concentration increasing from 5 mg/L to 25 mg/L. The reason was high concentrations of RhB molecules may cover the active site of the catalyst with limited light penetration. It further prevented the generation of photoelectric charge, and the interaction of ROS with PMS (Han et al., 2020a). On the other hand, the amount of formed SO4 −• in the degradation process was constant since a fixed Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalyst and PMS, which further hindered the degradation efficiency.

Five different temperatures (27 °C, 32 °C, 37 °C, 42 °C, 47 °C) proceeded in the Fe3O4@CQDs + PMS + light system with the results plotted in Fig. 5(d). The RhB degradation efficiency increased prominently with rising reaction temperature, which suggested that the increase of the reaction temperature favored the degradation of RhB. This phenomenon also can be explained as increasing the reaction temperature promoted the PMS to produce more SO4 −• (Eq. (5).). Furthermore, these results presented the system as an endothermic reaction, which could be substantially accelerated efficiency at elevated temperature. However, higher temperatures mean higher energy consumption, so costs should be considered in practical applications.
(5) Heat + HSO5 − → SO4 −•

The initial pH of the RhB could have a dramatic effect on the activation of PMS. Consequently, the degradation efficiency was also investigated at initial pHs of RhB ranging from 1 to 11. As elucidated in Fig. 5(e), the efficiency of RhB amplified significantly with the pH decreased, and reached a peak value at pH 1. This was due to iron-containing catalysts that could react with H+ to generate more ROS under acidic conditions, which were more conducive to promoting the degradation of RhB. Besides, when the initial pH of the RhB degradation solution was lower than the zero-charge (pHpzc) potential of the catalyst, it showed a positive surface charge. That helped to absorb more SO4 −• for the degradation of wastewater pollutants (Shuchi et al., 2020). However, the degradation rate of RhB under alkaline conditions is low because of the iron ion precipitate with OH−. Furthermore, SO4 −• would mainly react with OH− to produce •OH, which could further react with OH− to form H2O. It resulted in the reduction of free radicals and reduce the degradation efficiency of RhB (Ma et al., 2019).

To simulate the wastewater pollutants in reality, different coexisting anions (Cl−, H2PO4 −, HCO3 −, C2O4 2− and NO3 −) were added separately to investigate necessarily the effects of RhB degradation performance in the Fe3O4@CQDs + PMS + light system (Fig. 5(f)). Noticeably, it would be found that Cl− and NO3 − had a slight synergistic effect, which might be due to Cl− in the solution reacting with SO4•- and •OH to form reactive chlorine species (ClHO·-, Cl·) (Bekris et al., 2017; Jin et al., 2020) (Eqs. (6) and (7)), and NO3 − could significantly enhance the system because it formed additional active substances through photolysis (Ding et al., 2020) (Eqs. (8)–(10)). HCO3 − had a serious inhibition in the system (Boucheloukh et al., 2012; Xie, R. et al., 2021) (Eqs. (11)–(14)). The mechanism was excessive HCO3 − could competitively react with SO4 −• and •OH to produce CO3•-, which was a moderate oxidant. Analogously, H2PO4 − consumed ROS produced in the AOPs system, it could block the active sites on the catalyst surface and prevent to penetration of photons. Secondly, H2PO4 − formed a complex between PMS and Fe3O4@CQDs to reduce the degradation efficiency (Li et al., 2022; Luo et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2022b) (Eqs. (15) and (16)). The addition of C2O4 2− showed a strong inhibitory effect, this might be because C2O4 2− consumed the ROS resulting in almost no degradation of RhB after 30 min.
(6) Cl−/ClO3 −+ •OH → ClHO − •
(7) ClHO• - → Cl• + •OH
(8) NO3 − + hv → [NO3 −]* → OONO−
(9) [NO3 −]*→ NO2 − + O (3P)
(10) [NO3 −]*→ NO2• + O• - → NO2• + •OH + OH−
(11) CO3 2− + SO4 −•→ CO3 −• + SO4 2−
(12) •OH + CO3 2−→ CO3 −• + OH−
(13) SO4 −•+ HCO3 −→ SO4 2− + CO3 −• + H+
(14) •OH + HCO3 −→ CO3•- + H2O
(15) H2PO4 − + •OH → OH− + H2 + HPO4 −•
(16) H2PO4 − + SO4 −• → SO4 2− + HPO4 −•

HA widely exists in real surface water (Duan et al., 2019). Therefore, the effect of HA was researched in the Fe3O4@CQDs + PMS + light system. Fig. 5(g) depicts RhB efficiency improved as HA concentration enhancement, the fact is that functional groups in HA (hydroquinone, quinones and phenols) can induce the production of semiquinone radicals, accelerating the activation of PMS into •OH and SO4 −•. It elucidated that the Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalysts have excellent performance and are more suitable for treating real wastewater pollutants.

In order to evaluate the versatility of Fe3O4@CQDs + PMS + light system, the degradation capacity of different wastewater pollutants such as methylene blue (MB), tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), and levofloxacin (LEVX) was also investigated (Fig. 5(h)). The degradation efficiency of both MB and TC can reach 94% within 60 min, while the degradation efficiency of LEVX can also reach 70%. Therefore, the excellent application capability of Fe3O4@CQDs + PMS + light system was proved.

It is necessary to research the catalytic activity and performance of catalysts during long-term use. As depicted in Fig. 6 (a), four repeated experiments for RhB degradation were performed to confirm the practical value of the Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalyst. Four cycle degradation efficiency of RhB can reach more than 98%, which proved that the Fe3O4@CQDs + PMS + light system had excellent stability. The increased degradation time is because of the adsorption of RhB byproducts on the catalyst surface, which leads to the blockage of the active site. As can be presented in Fig. 6(b), the Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalyst is magnetic with easy to recycle. The results suggested that Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalyst could be thought of as an excellent catalytic material with better catalytic performance, stability and reusability. These features are of remarkable meaning in practical application. This reaction system is compared with the previously reported studies on PMS and visible light systems, presented in Table 1 . Lastly, it is confirmed that the Fe3O4@CQDs + PMS + light system has the unique advantages of a fast reaction rate, high degradation efficiency and excellent cycling performance. On the other hand, the catalyst is magnetic and easy to recover, and the dosage of the catalyst recovery is more than that of the other catalysts.

Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalyst presented excellent properties of activating PMS to produce ROS, while α-Fe2O3 can hardly activate PMS to produce ROS. This is because Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalyst showed strong charge transfer ability under visible light, and Fe(Ⅱ) in the catalyst can activate PMS. The corresponding DFT calculation was carried out to verify this hypothesis.

The adsorption model is used to describe the effect of the catalyst on the ROS generation of PMS activation and to predict the electronic changes during the degradation reaction. The α-Fe2O3 (110) and Fe3O4@CQDs (311) planes were selected based on the results of TEM and XRD analysis, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 7 (a), (b) and (c) the adsorption energy (ads) is −4.600 eV (α-Fe2O3), 4.644 eV (Fe3O4) and −5.247 eV (Fe3O4@CQDs), respectively. It can be observed that the adsorption energy of α-Fe2O3 (110) and Fe3O4 (311) is substantially the same, and the adsorption energy of CQDs is much higher than the first two after doping. This situation is possibly Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalyst exposing more active sites, which is more conducive to the adsorption of PMS, and PMS are more easily activated in the following reaction. Moreover, CQDs accelerated electron transfer within the system.

Fig. 7 (d), (e) and (f) showed the difference in charge densities, where the yellow and cyan regions are used for charge accumulation and depletion. The isoline distribution area of charge density difference is not as wide as that of Fe3O4 (311) and Fe3O4@CQDs (311) when α-Fe2O3 (110) is adsorbed on PMS, indicating that it has limited adsorption capacity relative to the other two adsorptions PMS. The electron enrichment and charge redistribution were stronger, and more electrons were transferred in the Fe3O4@CQDs (311) system. It is presented that PMS has excellent chemisorption on the surface composed of Fe3O4@CQDs (311). Specifically, the doping of CQDs leads to the bond formation and redistribution of the internal charge distribution, which favors the transport and transition of e− (Song et al., 2023).

The Bader charge populational analysis of PMS is shown in the Supplementary Material Fig. S3 and Table S2, respectively. O3 is the saturated oxygen atom, O1, O2, and O4 are the unsaturated oxygen atoms. It illustrated from the Supplementary Material Table S2 that ΔQ (e−) of O3 is 0.37, 0.03 and 0.02 in α-Fe2O3 (110), Fe3O4 (311) and Fe3O4@CQDs (311), among which Fe3O4@CQDs (311) value is the smallest. This attribute to the smaller the difference the more easily O3 is separated, and in contact with the active site of iron ion in the catalyst and generates ROS (Zhang et al., 2023). The ΔQ (e−) of S are −2.33, −2.35 and −1.96, respectively. It presented that the ΔQ (e−) of S increases significantly after the introduction of CQDs, which is due to the enhanced ability of S to take electrons from Fe, promoting stronger interaction between unsaturated oxygen and Fe.

DOS and PDOS were investigated through α-Fe2O3 (110), Fe3O4 (311), and Fe3O4@CQDs (311) before and after adsorption to explore the electron changes at the interface (Fig. 8 ). In contrast to the unmodified α-Fe2O3, Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@CQDs retain their metallic properties. Besides, the characteristic peaks in the DOS spectrum of Fe3O4@CQDs move towards the Fermi level, increasing the number of conducting carriers (Liu et al., 2023). Therefore, the embedding of CQDs effectively regulates the electron transfer efficiency at the catalyst interface and promotes conductivity.
The above DFT calculation results presented that Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalysts exhibited excellent catalytic performance.

Generally, ROS are responsible for the degradation of pollutants in the Fe3O4@CQDs + PMS + light system. To reveal the possible ROS generated in this system, the quenching experiments were produced in detail. Methanol (MeOH) is used to quench both •OH and SO4 −•, while tert butyl alcohol (TBA) can only quench •OH (Chen, Z. et al., 2020). Thereby, SO4 −• and •OH can be distinguished by MeOH and TBA. As described in Fig. 9 (a), the degradation of RhB was sectionally inhibited by the addition of 0.3 mol/L TBA and the inhibition was significantly increased with 0.6 mol/L TBA added. At both low (0.3 mol/L) and high (0.6 mol/L) concentrations, MeOH always inhibited RhB decomposition more than TBA. This is because, during the activation of PMS, low concentration of MeOH and TBA process reduces the generation of ROS on the surface of Fe3O4@CQDs catalyst. The high concentration of TBA and MeOH can mask the catalyst surface. It also indirectly indicated that the SO4 −• played a leading role in the system.

In addition, EDTA-2Na was selected as the quencher for the hole effect (h +) (Chen et al., 2022). As shown in Fig. 9(b), the inhibition effect on the reaction was obvious, because EDTA-2Na prevented the reaction between the hole and the water molecule from producing •OH (Chen et al., 2020). Besides, p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) and 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) were selected as quenchers of O2•- and 1O2, respectively (Wu et al., 2019). The degradation of RhB was inhibited with different concentrations of p-BQ and TEMP added, and the inhibition effect became more and more obvious with the increase in concentration. Based on the above results, it can be concluded that SO4•-, •OH, O2•- free radical pathways, and 1O2, h + non-free radical pathways were all involved in the degradation of RhB in varying degrees.

To further verify the mechanism involved in the RhB degradation, the EPR tests were performed by using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO). As depicted in Fig. 9(c) and (d), the SO4 −•, •OH and O2•- signals were the strongest with both catalyst and PMS coexisting, indicating that the Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalyst could activate PMS to produce SO4 −•, •OH and O2•-. The formation of oxygen vacancies in the reaction can be observed in the EPR test. The hole effect was the most significant in Fe3O4@CQDs + PMS + light system (Fig. 9(e)). The results displayed that the addition of visible light promoted the formation of defect sites and improved photocatalytic activity.

To further determine the mechanism in the Fe3O4@CQDs + PMS + light system. The chemical state and composition of the surface elements of Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalyst before and after the reaction were analyzed by XPS, as shown in Fig. 10 (a). The XPS spectra showed the diffraction peaks of C, O and Fe elements, but they did not change dramatically before and after the reaction. Hence, it is necessary to ulteriorly analyze the high-resolution XPS spectra of the above elements.

As demonstrated in Fig. 10(b), the C 1s spectra of Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalyst could be fitted into three peaks at 284.63 eV, 285.83 eV and 288.26 eV before the reaction. The characteristic peaks of 284.63 eV were associated with the C=C bond, 285.83 eV and 288.26 eV corresponded to the C–C bond and C=O respectively in the Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalyst, proving the existence of carbon quantum dots (Xie, X. et al., 2021). However, the characteristic peak of C 1s shifted obviously after use, and a new characteristic peak appeared at 292.44 eV because of the photocatalysis.

Fig. 10(c) depicted the O 1s XPS spectrum of the Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalyst with two peaks of 529.83 eV and 531.22 eV, which corresponded to lattice oxygen of metal oxides and surface-adsorbed oxygen species, respectively (Wu et al., 2021). The content of adsorbed oxygen increased from 43.6% to 47.02%, while the content of lattice oxygen (Fe–O) decreased from 56.4% to 52.98%. This phenomenon might be caused by the increase of ROS content in the PMS activated by the Fe–O bond during degradation.

The characteristic peak of Fe 2p was almost unchanged before and after the reaction (Fig. 10(d)). The two prominent signal peaks of Fe 2p 3/2 were located at 710.58 eV and 710.58 eV before and after the reaction, and the characteristic peaks of Fe 2p 2/1 were located at 723.38 eV and 723.98 eV, respectively, of which 719.17 eV and 718.93 eV were the satellite peaks (Lei et al., 2021; Wang, Q. et al., 2022). The proportion of Fe(Ⅲ) decreased from 36.31% to 32.28%, and Fe(Ⅱ) increased from 59.28% to 63.6% after the reaction. It could be inferred that Fe(Ⅲ) and Fe(Ⅱ) have participated in the reaction with PMS.

Combined with the results of DFT calculations, quenching experiments and EPR tests which suggested that free radical pathway SO4•-, •OH, O2•- and non-free radical pathway 1O2, h + participated and accelerated in the degradation of pollutants in the Fe3O4@CQDs + PMS + light system. The reaction mechanism is summarized and presented in Fig. 11 , which can be depicted as Fe(Ⅱ) activated PMS to generate SO4 −• and Fe(Ⅲ), and Fe(Ⅲ) was reduced to Fe(Ⅱ) through photoelectron interaction under visible light. CQDs on catalysts serve as the active site to enhance the absorption of visible light through the effective separation of photogenerated electrons. Besides, CQDs can effectively capture photoelectrons and accelerate charge transfer. Under the synergistic action of PMS and visible light, the degradation of RhB was accelerated by increasing the Fe(Ⅲ)/Fe(Ⅱ) cycling efficiency, as shown in Eqs. (17)–(26) (Chen et al., 2021; Han et al., 2020b; Ming et al., 2021; Tuna and Simsek, 2023).
(17) HSO5 − + eCB − → SO4 −• +OH−
(18) SO4 −• +H2O → HSO5 − + •OH+ H+
(19) CQDs + hv → hVB + + eCB −
(20) hVB + + H2O → •OH + H+
(21) CQDs + eCB − → •OH
(22) Fe(Ⅱ) + HSO5 − → Fe(Ⅲ) + SO4 −•
(23) Fe(Ⅱ) + HSO5 − → Fe(Ⅲ) + SO4 2− +1O2
(24) Fe(Ⅲ) + hv + H2O → Fe(Ⅱ) + •OH+ H+
(25) O2 + eCB − → O2 −•
(26) SO4 −•/•OH/O2•−/1O2/h + +RhB → CO2 + H2O + Small molecular

In this study, Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalysts with magnetic properties, high efficiency, and recovery value were prepared by the hydrothermal method. CQDs was embedded on the surface of the nanocatalyst, which accelerated the electron transfer and promoted the transformation of Fe(Ⅲ) to Fe(Ⅱ). It presented excellent degradation performance of RhB with Fe(Ⅲ)/Fe(Ⅱ) cycles formed under the synergistic effect of visible light and PMS, and its degradation efficiency reached 98% within 35 min. Additionally, the catalyst had good reusable performance, and the degradation efficiency of RhB can still reach 98% after 4 cycles. The adsorption energy reached −5.247 eV with Fe3O4@CQDs adsorbed PMS by DFT calculation. Meanwhile, DFT calculations also clearly demonstrated and confirmed that electrons were transferred more efficiently within the Fe3O4@CQDs nanocatalyst to activate PMS. The quenching experiments and EPR tests jointly verified that SO4•-, •OH, O2•- free radical pathway and 1O2, h + non-free radical pathway participated in the degradation of RhB in the Fe3O4@CQDs + PMS + light system. This study provides a scientific value for RhB degradation.
